title: Custom strategies
text: Adding strategy requires work sides: C++: Implement strategy class (computation). R: Write constructor function (user-facing API).

title: Custom strategies
text: strategy family base class pure virtual methods. new strategy inherits appropriate base implements . example, new projection pursuit strategy needs implement optimize() (find best projection): key pieces: to_json() serializes strategy name parameters. used model persistence. from_json() deserializes JSON validates unexpected keys present. PPFOREST2_REGISTER_STRATEGY registers factory JSON deserialization finds automatically. display_name() returns human-readable label summaries. Factory function (my_method()) convenience wrapper. pattern applies variable selection strategies (select()), cutpoint strategies (cutpoint()), strategy families. See C++ documentation complete interface definitions examples. writing .cpp file, add core/src/models/CMakeLists.txt.
code: // File: core/src/models/strategies/pp/MyMethod.hpp #pragma once #include "models/strategies/pp/ProjectionPursuit.hpp" #include "models/strategies/Strategy.hpp" #include "utils/JsonValidation.hpp"  namespace ppforest2::pp {  struct MyMethod : public ProjectionPursuit {   explicit MyMethod(float alpha) : alpha_(alpha) {}    std::string display_name() const override { return "My method"; }    Result optimize(     const types::FeatureMatrix&  x,     const stats::GroupPartition& group_spec) const override {     // Find the optimal projector for the data.     // Return Result{ projector_vector, index_value }.     ...   }    nlohmann::json to_json() const override {     return {{"name", "my_method"}, {"alpha", alpha_}};   }    static ProjectionPursuit::Ptr from_json(const nlohmann::json& j) {     validate_json_keys(j, "my_method PP", {"name", "alpha"});     return my_method(j.at("alpha").get<float>());   }    PPFOREST2_REGISTER_STRATEGY(ProjectionPursuit, "my_method")  private:   const float alpha_; };  inline ProjectionPursuit::Ptr my_method(float alpha) {   return std::make_shared<MyMethod>(alpha); }  }  // namespace ppforest2::pp

title: Custom strategies
text: Write R function creates strategy list. name field must match C++ registration name exactly. constructor : Validate parameters reach C++. Catching errors early clear messages better C++ exception. Set S3 class pp_strategy, vars_strategy, cutpoint_strategy, stop_strategy, binarize_strategy, partition_strategy. checked resolve_strategies(). Include display_name readable output summary(). Use parameter names to_json() C++. R list converted JSON passed from_json() C++ side.
code: #' My custom projection pursuit strategy. #' #' @param alpha A tuning parameter. #' @return A \code{pp_strategy} object. #' @export pp_my_method <- function(alpha = 1.0) {   if (!is.numeric(alpha) || length(alpha) != 1)     stop("`alpha` must be a single number.")    structure(     list(name = "my_method", display_name = "My method", alpha = alpha),     class = "pp_strategy"   ) }

title: Custom strategies
text: Create core/src/models/strategies/<family>/MyStrategy.hpp (.cpp needed). Inherit appropriate base class (ProjectionPursuit, VariableSelection, SplitCutpoint, StopRule, Binarization, StepPartition). Implement pure virtual methods. Implement to_json() "name" field. Implement display_name() human-readable summaries. Add static Ptr from_json() key validation. Add PPFOREST2_REGISTER_STRATEGY(Base, "name"). Add factory function strategy’s namespace. Add .cpp core/src/models/CMakeLists.txt. Write tests MyStrategy.test.cpp (JSON round-trip + functional). Write R constructor function validation, display_name, correct S3 class. Export document R function.

title: Introduction to ppforest2
text: Train projection-pursuit tree iris dataset: tree splits linear projections features. Use summary() see variable importance: Predict new observations:
code: library(ppforest2) #>  #> Attaching package: 'ppforest2' #> The following object is masked from 'package:datasets': #>  #>     iris  tree <- pptr(Type ~ ., data = iris, seed = 0) tree #>  #> Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree: #> If ([ 0.01 0.04 -0.04 -0.01 ] * x) < 0.06660754: #>  If ([ 0.04 0.07 -0.09 -0.15 ] * x) < -0.2075133: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  Else: #>    Predict: versicolor  #> Else: #>   Predict: setosa summary(tree) #>  #> Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree #>  #> pp method: LDA (lambda=0) #> vars method: All variables #> cutpoint method: Mean of means #> stop rule: Pure node #> binarize method: Largest gap #> partition method: By group #> leaf method: Majority vote #>  #>  #> Data Summary: #>   observations: 150  #>   features:     4  #>   groups:       3  #>   group names:  setosa, versicolor, virginica  #>   formula:      Type ~ Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width + Petal.Length + Petal.Width -      1  #>  #> Confusion Matrix: #>  #>             Predicted #> Actual       setosa versicolor virginica #>   setosa         50          0         0 #>   versicolor      0         48         2 #>   virginica       0          1        49 #>  #> Training error: 2% #>  #> Variable Importance: #>  #>       Variable         σ Projection #> 1 Petal.Length 1.7652982 0.10064526 #> 2  Petal.Width 0.7622377 0.06144172 #> 3 Sepal.Length 0.8280661 0.02164980 #> 4  Sepal.Width 0.4358663 0.02155388 #>  #> Note: Variable importance was calculated using scaled coefficients (|a_j| * σ_j). #> Variable contributions can only be theoretically interpreted as such #> if the model was trained on scaled data. Scaling also changes the #> projection-pursuit optimization, which may affect the resulting tree. preds <- predict(tree, iris) table(predicted = preds, actual = iris$Type) #>             actual #> predicted    setosa versicolor virginica #>   setosa         50          0         0 #>   versicolor      0         48         1 #>   virginica       0          2        49

title: Introduction to ppforest2
text: Train forest 100 trees, considering 2 randomly chosen variables split: summary shows OOB (--bag) error estimate three variable importance measures: Projection: average absolute projection coefficient across splits Weighted: projection importance weighted number observations routed node Permuted: decrease OOB accuracy variable permuted Predict class probabilities:
code: forest <- pprf(Type ~ ., data = iris, size = 100, n_vars = 2, seed = 0) summary(forest) #>  #> Random Forest of Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree #>  #> Size: 100 trees #> pp method: LDA (lambda=0) #> vars method: Uniform random (count=2) #> cutpoint method: Mean of means #> stop rule: Pure node #> binarize method: Largest gap #> partition method: By group #> leaf method: Majority vote #>  #>  #> Data Summary: #>   observations: 150  #>   features:     4  #>   groups:       3  #>   group names:  setosa, versicolor, virginica  #>   formula:      Type ~ Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width + Petal.Length + Petal.Width -      1  #>  #> Training Confusion Matrix: #>  #>             Predicted #> Actual       setosa versicolor virginica #>   setosa         50          0         0 #>   versicolor      0         48         2 #>   virginica       0          5        45 #>  #> Training error: 4.67% #>  #> OOB Confusion Matrix: #>  #>             Predicted #> Actual       setosa versicolor virginica #>   setosa         50          0         0 #>   versicolor      0         48         2 #>   virginica       0          4        46 #>  #> OOB error: 4% #>  #> Variable Importance: #>  #>       Variable         σ Projection    Weighted   Permuted #> 1 Petal.Length 1.7652982 0.07463032 0.057584818 0.33644474 #> 2  Petal.Width 0.7622377 0.04751209 0.036748469 0.24734434 #> 3 Sepal.Length 0.8280661 0.02340555 0.013667455 0.08662503 #> 4  Sepal.Width 0.4358663 0.01040520 0.008798525 0.04265482 #>  #> Note: Variable importance was calculated using scaled coefficients (|a_j| * σ_j). #> Variable contributions can only be theoretically interpreted as such #> if the model was trained on scaled data. Scaling also changes the #> projection-pursuit optimization, which may affect the resulting tree. probs <- predict(forest, iris[1:5, ], type = "prob") probs #>   setosa versicolor virginica #> 1      1          0         0 #> 2      1          0         0 #> 3      1          0         0 #> 4      1          0         0 #> 5      1          0         0

title: Introduction to ppforest2
text: control, can pass strategy objects directly instead using shortcut parameters (lambda, n_vars, p_vars). equivalent makes strategy choice explicit: Available strategy constructors: pp_pda(lambda) — PDA projection pursuit (lambda = 0 LDA) vars_uniform(n_vars) vars_uniform(p_vars) — random variable selection vars_all() — use variables (default single trees) cutpoint_mean_of_means() — midpoint split rule (default) Strategy objects can also passed engine arguments parsnip:
code: # These two calls produce identical results: forest_shortcut <- pprf(Type ~ ., data = iris, size = 10, lambda = 0.5, n_vars = 2, seed = 0)  forest_explicit <- pprf(Type ~ ., data = iris, size = 10, pp = pp_pda(0.5), vars = vars_uniform(n_vars = 2), seed = 0)  all.equal(predict(forest_shortcut, iris), predict(forest_explicit, iris)) #> [1] TRUE library(parsnip)  spec <- pp_rand_forest(trees = 10) |>   set_engine("ppforest2", pp = pp_pda(0.5), vars = vars_uniform(n_vars = 2)) |>   set_mode("classification")  fit <- spec |> fit(Type ~ ., data = iris) predict(fit, iris[1:5, ]) #> # A tibble: 5 × 1 #>   .pred_class #>   <fct>       #> 1 setosa      #> 2 setosa      #> 3 setosa      #> 4 setosa      #> 5 setosa

title: Introduction to ppforest2
text: ppforest2 integrates tidymodels ecosystem via parsnip:
code: library(parsnip)  # Single tree spec <- pp_tree(penalty = 0.5) |>   set_engine("ppforest2") |>   set_mode("classification")  fit <- spec |> fit(Type ~ ., data = iris) predict(fit, iris[1:5, ]) #> # A tibble: 5 × 1 #>   .pred_class #>   <fct>       #> 1 setosa      #> 2 setosa      #> 3 setosa      #> 4 setosa      #> 5 setosa # Random forest spec <- pp_rand_forest(trees = 50, mtry = 2, penalty = 0.5) |>   set_engine("ppforest2") |>   set_mode("classification")  fit <- spec |> fit(Type ~ ., data = iris) predict(fit, iris[1:5, ], type = "prob") #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>   .pred_setosa .pred_versicolor .pred_virginica #>          <dbl>            <dbl>           <dbl> #> 1            1                0               0 #> 2            1                0               0 #> 3            1                0               0 #> 4            1                0               0 #> 5            1                0               0

what: Projection Pursuit Oblique Decision Trees and Random Forests
title: Projection Pursuit Oblique Decision Trees and Random Forests
text: ppforest2 provides projection pursuit oblique decision trees random forests classification. Instead splitting single variables, node projects data onto linear combination features, capturing structure axis-aligned trees miss. package wraps high-performance C++ core intended modern successor PPforest. Key capabilities: oblique splits via projection pursuit, multi-threaded forest training (OpenMP), cross-platform reproducibility, three variable importance measures (projection-based, weighted, permutation), LDA/PDA optimisation, OOB error estimation, parsnip / tidymodels integration.

title: Trains a Random Forest of Project-Pursuit oblique decision trees. — pprf
text: function trains Random Forest Project-Pursuit oblique decision tree using either formula data frame interface matrix-based interface. using formula interface, specify model formula data frame containing variables. matrix-based interface, provide matrices features labels directly. number trees controlled size parameter. tree trained stratified bootstrap sample drawn data. number variables consider split controlled n_vars parameter. lambda = 0, model trained using Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA). lambda > 0, model trained using Penalized Discriminant Analysis (PDA).

code: pprf(   formula = NULL,   data = NULL,   x = NULL,   y = NULL,   size = 2,   lambda = 0,   n_vars = NULL,   p_vars = NULL,   seed = NULL,   max_retries = 3L,   threads = NULL,   pp = NULL,   vars = NULL,   cutpoint = NULL,   stop = NULL,   binarize = NULL,   partition = NULL,   leaf = NULL )

title: Trains a Random Forest of Project-Pursuit oblique decision trees. — pprf
text: formula formula form y ~ x1 + x2 + ..., y vector labels x1, x2, ... features. data data frame containing variables formula. x matrix containing features observation. y matrix containing labels observation. size number trees forest. lambda regularization parameter. lambda = 0, model trained using Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA). lambda > 0, model trained using Penalized Discriminant Analysis (PDA). used together pp. n_vars number variables consider split (integer). chosen uniformly split. default variables. used together p_vars dr. p_vars proportion variables consider split (number 0 1, exclusive). example, p_vars = 0.5 uses half features. used together n_vars dr. seed optional integer seed reproducibility. NULL (default), seed drawn R's RNG, set.seed() controls reproducibility. integer provided, value used directly. seed used training computing permuted variable importance. max_retries Maximum number retries degenerate trees (default: 3). bootstrap sample yields singular covariance matrix, tree retrained different seed many times. threads number threads use. default number cores available. pp projection pursuit strategy object created pp_pda. used together lambda. vars variable selection strategy object created vars_uniform vars_all. used together n_vars p_vars. cutpoint split cutpoint strategy object created cutpoint_mean_of_means (default). stop stopping rule object created stop_pure_node (default). binarize binarization strategy object created binarize_largest_gap (default). partition partition strategy object created partition_by_group (default). leaf leaf strategy object created leaf_majority_vote (default).

title: ppforest2 0.1.0
text: Core: Projection-pursuit oblique decision trees random forests classification, using LDA/PDA optimization. Core: Random uniform variable selection per split forest diversity. Core: Three variable importance measures: permuted (VI1), projections (VI2), weighted projections (VI3). Core: --bag error confusion matrix forests, bootstrap sample indices persisted recomputation. Core: Degenerate split detection projection-pursuit find useful projection. Core: OpenMP multi-threaded forest training. Core: JSON serialization trained models. Core: Cross-platform reproducibility: identical results seed Linux, macOS, Windows. R: pptr() pprf() formula matrix interfaces. R: predict() group labels (type = "class") vote proportions (type = "prob"). R: Training OOB confusion matrices displayed summary(). R: Degenerate split warnings projection-pursuit separate groups. R: save_json() load_json() model persistence. R: tidymodels/parsnip integration: pp_tree() pp_rand_forest() model specifications. R: ggplot2 visualizations: tree diagrams, variable importance plots, projection histograms, decision boundary plots. R: Bundled datasets: crab, crabs, fishcatch, glass, image, iris, leukemia, lymphoma, NCI60, olive, parkinson, wine. CLI: train: fit tree forest CSV, save JSON. CLI: predict: classify new data saved model. CLI: evaluate: train/test evaluation smart convergence. CLI: summarize: display model configuration, data summary, metrics saved model JSON. Supports --data recompute metrics training data. CLI: benchmark: multi-scenario performance benchmarks baseline comparison.
